_title: BS 7671:2018+A2:2022 Appendix 4 — Aluminium Conductor Cable Ratings
_note: Aluminium conductors have approximately 65% the conductivity of copper. Same physical size has lower current rating. Equivalent rating typically requires 1 step larger cross-section vs copper.

common: DNO service cables (consumer service from network) — typically aluminium for cost
Very large submains in commercial/industrial (typically > 95mm² copper threshold)
Transformer LV connections (large flexible busbars often aluminium)
Underground HV cables (16/95mm² Wavecon equivalent and larger)
avoid_for: Final circuits — too brittle and large for typical wiring accessories
Sizes below 16mm² — cost saving is negligible, termination issues outweigh
Direct connection to copper without bimetallic interface — galvanic corrosion

termination_requirements.antioxidant_grease: Required at all terminations — prevents oxide film build-up
termination_requirements.bimetallic_lugs: When terminating to copper busbars/terminals — copper-aluminium bimetallic plate or compression lug
termination_requirements.torque: Use manufacturer-specified torque — aluminium creeps under sustained pressure
termination_requirements.retorque_intervals: Initial install + 1 year + every 5 years (industrial good practice — not BS 7671 mandatory)

table_4e2a_aluminium_xlpe_single_core._reference: BS 7671:2018 Table 4E2A — 90°C thermosetting insulated, single-core, aluminium
table_4e2a_aluminium_xlpe_single_core._note: Ratings ~80% of equivalent copper. Voltage drop is higher (resistance proportional to 1/conductivity).

method_C._formation: Two single-core cables flat and touching, clipped direct

method_E_trefoil._formation: Three single-core cables in trefoil, free air or cable tray

table_4e4a_aluminium_swa_xlpe_multicore._reference: BS 7671:2018 Table 4E4A — 90°C thermosetting, armoured, multi-core, aluminium

cross_section_equivalence_to_copper._note: Approximate rule: aluminium needs ~1.6× the cross-section of copper for the same rating

design_considerations.voltage_drop_penalty: Aluminium voltage drop is approximately 1.6× copper for the same cross-section. Often dominates the sizing decision.
design_considerations.weight_advantage: Aluminium is approximately 30% the weight of copper for the same cross-section — significant for very large submains and risers.
design_considerations.cost_advantage: Aluminium cable cost per metre is approximately 40-50% of copper at large sizes (>95mm²). Below that, savings are eroded by termination cost.
design_considerations.thermal_expansion: Aluminium has higher thermal expansion than copper — terminations must accommodate cyclic movement.
design_considerations.creep: Aluminium under sustained mechanical pressure deforms slowly (creep). Re-torque required periodically.
design_considerations.fault_performance: Aluminium has lower k value (94 vs 143 for copper/XLPE) — adiabatic check is more onerous. May force larger cross-section for high-fault circuits.

correction_factors_apply_same._note: All correction factors from appendix4-correction-factors.json apply equally to aluminium. Ca, Cg, Ci, Cd values are independent of conductor material.
correction_factors_apply_same._caveat: k value (adiabatic constant) differs — see reg434-fault-current.json. Use aluminium k values for adiabatic check.
